title: Seventy-Nine
content:
آ آ آ آ Know me next time when you see me, wonï؟½t you, old smarty?
آ آ آ آ Oh, I mean you, old figger-head, just the same party!
آ آ آ آ Take out your pensivil, d n you; sharpen it, do!
آ آ آ آ Any complaints to make? Lots of ï؟½em one of ï؟½emï؟½s you.

آ آ آ آ You! who are you, anyhow, goinï؟½ round in that sneakinï؟½ way?
آ آ آ آ Never in jail before, was you, old blatherskite, say?
آ آ آ آ Look at it; donï؟½t it look pooty? Oh, grin, and be d d to you, do!
آ آ آ آ But if I had you this side oï؟½ that gratin,ï؟½ Iï؟½d just make it lively for you.

آ آ آ آ How did I get in here? Well what ï؟½ud you give to know?
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twasnï؟½t by sneakinï؟½ round where I hadnï؟½t no call to go;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twasnï؟½t by hanginï؟½ round a-spyinï؟½ unfortnet men.
آ آ آ آ Grin! but Iï؟½ll stop your jaw if ever you do that agen.

آ آ آ آ Why donï؟½t you say suthin, blast you? Speak your mind if you dare.
آ آ آ آ Ainï؟½t I a bad lot, sonny? Say it, and call it square.
آ آ آ آ Hainï؟½t got no tongue, hey, hev ye? Oh, guard! hereï؟½s a little swell
آ آ آ آ A cussinï؟½ and swearinï؟½ and yellinï؟½, and bribinï؟½ me not to tell.

آ آ آ آ There! I thought that ï؟½ud fetch ye! And you want to know my name?
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Seventy-nineï؟½ they call me, but that is their little game;
آ آ آ آ For Iï؟½m werry highly connected, as a gent, sir, can understand,
آ آ آ آ And my family hold their heads up with the very furst in the land.

آ آ آ آ For ï؟½twas all, sir, a put-up job on a pore young man like me;
آ آ آ آ And the jury was bribed a puppos, and at furst they couldnï؟½t agree;
آ آ آ آ And I sed to the judge, sez I, Oh, grin! itï؟½s all right, my son!
آ آ آ آ But youï؟½re a werry lively young pup, and you ainï؟½t to be played upon!

آ آ آ آ Wotï؟½s that you got? tobacco? Iï؟½m cussed but I thought ï؟½twas a tract.
آ آ آ آ Thank ye! A chap tï؟½other day now, lookee, this is a fact
آ آ آ آ Slings me a tract on the evils oï؟½ keepinï؟½ bad company,
آ آ آ آ As if all the saints was howlinï؟½ to stay here along oï؟½ we.

آ آ آ آ No, I hainï؟½t no complaints. Stop, yes; do you see that chap,
آ آ آ آ Him standinï؟½ over there, a-hidinï؟½ his eyes in his cap?
آ آ آ آ Well, that manï؟½s stumick is weak, and he canï؟½t stand the prisï؟½n fare;
آ آ آ آ For the coffee is just half beans, and the sugar it ainï؟½t nowhere.

آ آ آ آ Perhaps itï؟½s his bringinï؟½ up; but heï؟½s sickeninï؟½ day by day,
آ آ آ آ And he doesnï؟½t take no food, and Iï؟½m seeinï؟½ him waste away.
آ آ آ آ And it isnï؟½t the thing to see; for, whatever heï؟½s been and done,
آ آ آ آ Starvation isnï؟½t the plan as heï؟½s to be saved upon.

آ آ آ آ For he cannot rough it like me; and he hasnï؟½t the stamps, I guess,
آ آ آ آ To buy him his extry grub outside oï؟½ the prisï؟½n mess.
آ آ آ آ And perhaps if a gent like you, with whom Iï؟½ve been sorter free,
آ آ آ آ Would thank you! But, say! look here! Oh, blast it! donï؟½t give it to me!

آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t you give it to me; now, donï؟½t ye, donï؟½t ye, donï؟½t!
آ آ آ آ You think itï؟½s a put-up job; so Iï؟½ll thank ye, sir, if you wonï؟½t.
آ آ آ آ But hand him the stamps yourself: why, he isnï؟½t even my pal;
آ آ آ آ And, if itï؟½s a comfort to you, why, I donï؟½t intend that he shall.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
